narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, an LSAT score of 35.50, and details about your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, along with your lower-middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 56.3%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction means the school views you as higher risk for not passing the bar initially, a key factor they use to ensure strong outcomes for their students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a potential dip in later academic momentum that often correlates with bar struggles. Counterbalancing that, your LSAT score had the second-strongest influence but in a positive direction, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 36.70 seen in successful students. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 worked in your favor as the third-most important factor, exceeding the typical 3.21 average and suggesting solid foundational skills. While your lower-middle family income and full-time status played smaller roles—common among applicants, with full-time being the norm for over 90%—they didn't sway the outcome much compared to your grades trajectory. Overall, your profile stands out as stronger than average in undergrad performance and testing, but the low Year 3 decile raised red flags when weighed against students who maintained or improved their rankings and went on to pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic student pursuing law, where many from similar backgrounds succeed with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year decile, perhaps through community college refreshers or targeted tutoring, and reapply after strengthening that area—many students in your position have turned predictions around this way. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your personal story more heavily alongside these metrics. Keep pushing; your above-average UGPA and solid LSAT show you're capable with the right adjustments.
